questions.text: The egg of a chick is laid at one of the following stages:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gastrula, early multicellular embryo, composed of two or more germinal layers of cells from which the various organs later derive.

Blastulation is the stage in early animal embryonic development that produces the blastula. In mammalian development and fertilization, the blastula develops into the blastocyst with a differentiated inner cell mass and an outer trophectoderm. The egg is laid 24 hours after fertilisation at the stage of the blastula. Hence, it is the correct option.
Cleavage facilitates the distribution of cytoplasm among the blastomeres.
The morula stage is usually defined as the stage in which the embryo consists of 16-32 cells.
Neurulation refers to the folding process in vertebrate embryos, which includes the transformation of the neural plate into the neural tube.

questions.explanation: The egg is laid 24 hours after fertilization at the stage of blastula, and its further development takes place, when the egg is incubated by the female. Incubation must continue steadily for 21 days at a temperature of 103°F. The blastula is surrounded by a protective layer called the zona pellucida. Additionally egg is laid after cleavage is completed but before gastrulation and blastula is the stage which perfectly fits in this criteria.

questions.text: Tails can be regenerated in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is partially correct. Lizards are able to regenerate their tails. While some species of amphibians can also regenerate their tails as larvae, they lose this ability as they metamorphose into adults.
Lizards have the ability to regenerate their tails along with some other species of amphibions.

options.text: Both lizards and the larvae of amphibians
options.explanation: Lizards are able to regenerate their tails. While some species of amphibians can also regenerate their tails as larvae, they lose this ability as they metamorphose into adults. Since both lizards and larvae are mentioned in this option so this option is correct.
Factually incorrect since option C provides the right answer

questions.explanation: Lizards possess the ability to regenerate a cartilage rod and associated tail from an already regenerated portion of their tail, after a shearing event through the cartilage rod, such as a bite from a predator. Various research papers suggest larvae also possess this ability.

questions.text: Germ cells give rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Legs are composed of muscle, bone, and connective tissues, which are not derived from germ cells. They develop from somatic cells, not germ cells.
The head is formed from various tissues including neural tissues, which originate from the neural ectoderm, not germ cells.
Germ cells differentiate into gametes, which are eggs in females and sperm in males. These are essential for sexual reproduction.
Hands are formed from limb buds consisting of mesodermal mesenchyme that differentiates into bones and muscles, not from germ cells.
Germ cells specifically give rise to gametes, not all body parts. Somatic cells form the various tissues and structures of the body.

questions.explanation: There are two types of human germ cells, eggs, and sperms. Germ cells are specialized cells that give rise to gametes, the reproductive cells that fuse during fertilization to create a new organism. In humans, germ cells are found in the gonads, the ovaries in females and the testes in males.

questions.text: When a fetus is in the uterus, what carries oxygen away from the placenta?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The amniotic fluid is a fluid-filled sac that surrounds the fetus. It protects the fetus from shock and provides it with a constant temperature.
The amniotic sac is a thin membrane that lines the amniotic fluid sac. It doesnot play any role in diffusion of nutrients and gases
The lining of the uterus is the tissue that lines the uterus and provides it with nutrients

The umbilical cord connects the baby to the mother's placenta. During fetal development in the womb, the umbilical cord is the lifeline to the baby supplying nutrients. After birth, the cord is clamped and cut. Eventually, between 1 to 3 weeks, the cord will become dry and will naturally fall off. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: The umbilical cord (Latin: Funiculus umbilicalis) is a tube-like structure that connects the developing fetus to the placenta. It has one umbilical vein and two umbilical arteries, which are used by the fetal heart to pump blood to and from the placenta, which exchanges nutrients and waste products with the mother's circulatory system.

questions.text: Germ layers are formed in which stage of development?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Morulation is the biological process where a fertilized egg (zygote) divides repeatedly through cleavage to form a solid, ball-like structure of cells called a morula. Morulation does not play any role in germ layer development
Blastulation is the process of early embryonic development where a solid ball of cells, the morula, transforms into a hollow, fluid-filled sphere called a blastula or, in mammals, a blastocyst.

Germ layers that are ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm are formed during gastrulation. During gastrulation, the blastoderm splits into two layers: an upper layer of cells called epiblast and a lower layer of cells called hypoblast. The epiblast is the presumptive ectoderm and mesoderm while the hypoblast is the presumptive endoderm.
In this stage organs are formed from the germ layers. it takes place after gastrulation.

questions.explanation: Germ layers that are ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm are formed during gastrulation. During gastrulation, the blastoderm splits into two layers: an upper layer of cells called epiblast and a lower layer of cells called hypoblast. The epiblast is the presumptive ectoderm and mesoderm while the hypoblast is the presumptive endoderm.

questions.text: Innermost microsporangial wall layer that nourishes pollen grains is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Endothecium develops fibrous thickenings at maturity, which are hygroscopic in nature and helps in anther dehiscence.

Tapetum is the innermost layer of the anther wall and surrounds the sporogenous tissue completely. It provides nutrition to the developing microspores which form the pollen grains. Hence, the innermost microsporangial wall that nourishes pollen grains is tapetum. So, the correct answer is B.

The endodermis (the innermost layer of the cortex adjacent to the pericycle) is composed of closely packed cells that have within their walls Casparian strips, water-impermeable deposits of suberin that regulate water and mineral uptake by the roots.
The function of sporogenous tissues is the production of pollen grains.

questions.explanation: The microsporangial wall comprises four layers: Epidermis, Endothecium, Middle layers and Tapetum .Tapetum is the innermost layer of the anther wall and surrounds the sporogenous tissue completely. It provides nutrition to the developing microspores which form the pollen grains.Hence, the innermost microsporangial wall that nourishes pollen grains is tapetum. So, the correct answer is B.

questions.text: The major sign and symptoms of microcephaly is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because sexual defects occur in diseases like klinefelter’s syndrome (male sexual defect) and turner’s syndrome (female sexual defect) but not microcephaly.
This option is incorrect because an excessive number of toes is called polydactyly.
This option is incorrect because mental retardation is not the major sign of microcephaly. It may be manifested in other conditions like Down’s Syndrome, Krabbe’s, Gaucher’s, Tay Sachs etc.

options.text: Small skull in proportion to the normal body size
options.explanation: Microcephaly is a condition of abnormal development in which the head is smaller than normal. It may be caused by deficiency of Growth Hormone secreted by the pituitary gland. Therefore, this option is the correct answer.

options.text: Split in upper lip and gap in the roof of mouth
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because split in upper lip and gap in roof of mouth is characteristic of cleft palate.

questions.explanation: Microcephaly is a neurological condition in which a baby's head is much smaller than expected for the baby's age. The head circumference is typically below the third percentile for the baby's sex and age. Microcephaly can be caused by a variety of genetic and environmental factors, and it can be associated with a range of developmental disabilities.

questions.text: Microcephaly, the small sized skull is due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nutritional errors may cause weakening of bones but it doesn't cause the deformation or size difference in bones.
Skeletal defects can cause several disorders but the root cause of microcephaly is genetic cause which is also a cause of many skeletal errors.
Hormones control the deposition of material in bones but they do not affect the size or their formation.
The formation of bones is influenced mainly by genes so it is likely that errors in the formation of bones are due to genetic defects.

questions.explanation: Microcephaly is a condition where the head (circumference) is smaller than normal. Microcephaly may be caused by genetic abnormalities. Genetic mutations in various genes can disrupt brain development and cause microcephaly. Some well-known genetic syndromes associated with microcephaly include Down syndrome, Apert syndrome, and Seckel syndrome.

questions.text: Cytoplasmic localization is a consequence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fertilization is the process where a sperm cell and an egg cell combine to form a zygote, marking the start of development in sexual reproduction. This complex process results in a unique combination of genes from both parents, creating a new individual with a full set of chromosomes.

Cleavage, in the context of embryology and development, refers to a series of rapid and successive cell divisions that occur in the early stages of the development of a multicellular organism, typically following fertilization. These cell divisions are essential for the formation of a multicellular structure from a single-celled zygote and give rise to a cluster of smaller, undifferentiated cells called blastomeres.

A morula is an early stage of embryonic development that follows several rounds of cell division after fertilization. It resembles a small, solid mass of cells, similar to a mulberry. The morula eventually transforms into a blastocyst
A blastula is an early stage of embryonic development in animals, following the morula stage. It is characterized by the formation of a fluid-filled cavity called the blastocoel.

questions.explanation: Cytoplasmic localization is a consequence of cleavage. Cleavage, in the context of embryology and development, refers to a series of rapid and successive cell divisions that occur in the early stages of the development of a multicellular organism, typically following fertilization. These cell divisions are essential for the formation of a multicellular structure from a single-celled zygote and give rise to a cluster of smaller, undifferentiated cells called blastomeres.Cleavage sets the stage for subsequent stages of embryonic development, including gastrulation, organogenesis, and differentiation.

questions.text: A bean seed contains all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The seed coat is the outer protective layer of a seed, shielding the embryonic plant inside.
The epicotyl is a part of the plant embryo in a seed that will develop into the shoot bearing the first true leaves.
The hypocotyl is the part of the embryo that becomes the stem below the seed leaves (cotyledons) and above the root.
A hypha is a filamentous structure found in fungi and not in seeds. Therefore, it is not present in a bean seed.
The cotyledon is a significant part of the seed, acting as a food storage structure to nourish the developing plant embryo.

questions.explanation: A seed coat is the outer protective layer of a seed. An epicotyl is the part of the embryo that grows above the ground. A hypocotyl is the part of the embryo that grows below the ground. A cotyledon is a leaf-like structure that stores food for the embryo. A hypha is a thread-like filament that is found in fungi.

questions.text: _ is the reconstruction of the lost part of the body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Growth refers to an increase in size and mass of an organism, not the reconstruction of lost parts. It involves cell division and enlargement leading to a permanent change in form.

Development encompasses the overall changes and progression of an organism through its life cycle, including differentiation and maturation of cells, but not specifically the reconstruction of lost body parts.

Regeneration is the biological process through which organisms reconstruct lost or damaged parts of their body, thereby restoring structure and function. This is the correct option as it directly addresses the question.
Blastulation is a phase in early embryonic development that forms the blastula. It is unrelated to the reconstruction of lost body parts.
Gastrulation is an embryonic process where the blastula reorganizes into a gastrula. It is involved in early development rather than reconstruction of lost body parts.

questions.explanation: Regeneration is the natural process of replacing or restoring damaged or missing cells, tissues, organs, and even entire body parts to full function in plants and animals. It is the ability of an organism to replace lost or damaged parts of its body.

questions.text: The disease that arises due to increasing age is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Older age doesn't cause Alzheimer's, but it is the most important known risk factor for the disease. The number of people with Alzheimer's disease doubles about every 5 years beyond age 65.

Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by the hepatitis C virus (HCV). Hepatitis C is spread through contact with blood from an infected person. Today, most people become infected with the hepatitis C virus by sharing needles or other equipment used to prepare and inject drugs.

Cholera is an acute, diarrheal illness caused by infection of the intestine with the toxigenic bacterium Vibrio cholerae serogroup 01 or 0139. An estimated 2.9 million cases and 95,000 deaths occur each year around the world. The infection is often mild or without symptoms, but it can be severe.

Malaria is a serious and sometimes fatal disease caused by a parasite that commonly infects a certain type of mosquito that feeds on humans. People who get malaria are typically very sick, with high fevers, shaking chills, and flu-like illnesses.
Beriberi is a nutritional deficiency. All other diseases are caused by pathogens, irrespective of age group.

questions.explanation: Alzheimer's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder that is characterized by progressive loss of memory, cognitive function, and behavior. It is the most common cause of dementia, accounting for about 60-80% of cases. The risk of Alzheimer's disease increases with age, and it is most common in people over the age of 65.

questions.text: The cells in our body are all genetically identical, apart from the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All cells in our body, except for reproductive cells, contain the same genetic material. This is because they all originate from a single fertilized egg cell, which undergoes multiple rounds of cell division to produce the various types of cells in the body.

This option is correct because Reproductive cells, however, are different. They are produced in the gonads (ovaries in females and testes in males) and are specialized for the purpose of reproduction.
Muscle fibres originate from somatic cells and all somatic cell are genetically identical.

All cells in our body, except for reproductive cells, contain the same genetic material. This is because they all originate from a single fertilized egg cell, which undergoes multiple rounds of cell division to produce the various types of cells in the body.

questions.explanation: Reproductive cells are different. They are produced in the gonads (ovaries in females and testes in males) and are specialized for the purpose of reproduction. In contrast to somatic cells, which have two copies of each chromosome (one from each parent), reproductive cells only have one copy of each chromosome. This is because they are produced through a process called meiosis, which involves two rounds of cell division. During meiosis, the homologous chromosomes (pairs of chromosomes that are identical in size and structure) are separated, resulting in cells with half the number of chromosomes as the original cell. This reduction in chromosome number is necessary for sexual reproduction, as it allows for the mixing of genetic material from two parents to create a new individual with a unique genetic makeup.

questions.text: The cells that play a vital role in the differentiation of various body parts are called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ectoderm is the outermost germ layer in the embryo. Ectodermal cells give rise to various tissues, such as the epidermis (outermost layer of the skin), hair, nails, tooth enamel, and parts of the nervous system, including the brain and spinal cord. They also differentiate into the sensory cells of the eyes, ears, and nose.

The mesoderm is the middle germ layer in the embryo. Mesodermal cells give rise to several important tissues and structures in the body, including muscles, bones, connective tissues, blood vessels, kidneys, heart, and reproductive organs. They also contribute to the development of the circulatory, skeletal, and excretory systems.

The endoderm is the innermost germ layer in the embryo. Endodermal cells give rise to the epithelial lining of various organs, including the digestive tract (stomach, intestines), respiratory system (lungs), liver, pancreas, and urinary bladder. They also contribute to the formation of glands, such as the thyroid and thymus.
This is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: During embryonic development, the cells in the early embryo undergo a process called differentiation, where they become specialized and give rise to different tissues and organs in the body. This process is controlled by specific groups of cells known as germ layers, which are formed during early embryonic development. These germ layers are the ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm.Ectodermal cells: The ectoderm is the outermost germ layer in the embryo. Ectodermal cells give rise to various tissues, such as the epidermis (outermost layer of the skin), hair, nails, tooth enamel, and parts of the nervous system, including the brain and spinal cord. They also differentiate into the sensory cells of the eyes, ears, and nose.Mesodermal cells: The mesoderm is the middle germ layer in the embryo. Mesodermal cells give rise to several important tissues and structures in the body, including muscles, bones, connective tissues, blood vessels, kidneys, heart, and reproductive organs. They also contribute to the development of the circulatory, skeletal, and excretory systems. Endodermal cells: The endoderm is the innermost germ layer in the embryo. Endodermal cells give rise to the epithelial lining of various organs, including the digestive tract (stomach, intestines), respiratory system (lungs), liver, pancreas, and urinary bladder. They also contribute to the formation of glands, such as the thyroid and thymus. Therefore, all three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm) play a vital role in the differentiation of various body parts during embryonic development.

questions.text: The individual with harelip shows the following conditions:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is wrong because The hard palate, which composes two-thirds of the total palate area, is a plate of bone covered by a moist, durable layer of mucous-membrane tissue, which secretes small amounts of mucus. This layer forms several ridges that help grip food while the tongue agitates it during chewing.

This option is wrong because Polydactyly is a condition in which a baby is born with one or more extra fingers. It is a common condition that often runs in families. The extra fingers are usually small and abnormally developed. Polydactyly is one of the most common congenital (present at birth) hand abnormalities.

This option is correct because In a few cases, cleft lip and palate are associated with harelip: the genes a child inherits from their parents (although most cases are a one-off) smoking during pregnancy or drinking alcohol during pregnancy. obesity during pregnancy.

This option is wrong because Microcephaly is a condition where a baby's head is much smaller than expected. During pregnancy, a baby's head grows because the baby's brain grows. Microcephaly can occur because a baby's brain has not developed properly during pregnancy or has stopped growing after birth, which results in a smaller head size.

questions.explanation: Harelip is a synonymous term for Cleft Palate. A cleft palate happens if the tissue that makes up the roof of the mouth does not join together completely during pregnancy.

questions.text: Amniotic fluid of human embryo protects it from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The amniotic fluid helps protect the developing embryo from degeneration. It provides a supportive environment that cushions the embryo, preventing mechanical damage and maintaining a stable temperature. Additionally, the fluid contains various nutrients, growth factors, and hormones that support the embryo's growth and development.

The amniotic fluid acts as a shock absorber, protecting the embryo from sudden movements or jerks that may occur within the mother's body. It helps to dampen external forces and reduces the risk of injury to the developing embryo. Hence, this option is correct.

The term "encasement" is not typically associated with the role of amniotic fluid. However, the amniotic sac, which is filled with amniotic fluid, does encase the embryo. The amniotic sac consists of the amnion (inner membrane) and the chorion (outer membrane) and provides a protective barrier around the developing embryo.
Factually incorrect since option B is correct

questions.explanation: The amniotic fluid acts as a shock absorber, protecting the embryo from sudden movements or jerks that may occur within the mother's body. It helps to dampen external forces and reduces the risk of injury to the developing embryo.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct sequence of missing events in the diagram given below:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This sequence is incorrect. Fertilization must occur first to form a zygote, followed by cleavage, gastrulation, and finally organogenesis.
This sequence is incorrect. Development starts with fertilization, creating a zygote, followed by cleavage, gastrulation, and then organogenesis.
This is the correct sequence. Fertilization results in a zygote, which undergoes cleavage. The process continues with gastrulation, then organogenesis.
This sequence is incorrect as it starts with gastrulation. The correct process begins with fertilization, followed by cleavage, gastrulation, and organogenesis.

questions.explanation: After gamete formation, the first process that occurs is fertilization, which means the fusion of gametes to form a zygote. Then this zygote divides which is termed as cleavage, then after that and before organogenesis, we have the gastrula stage. Hence option C is correct.

questions.text: Wood is not formed in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monocots are plants that have only one cotyledon in their seed, such as grasses, lilies, and orchids. They do not produce true wood like dicots and gymnosperms, which have a secondary growth in the stem resulting in the formation of woody tissues such as the xylem and phloem. Hence, this option is correct.
Dicotyledonous plants (dicots) are capable of forming wood.
Gymnosperms are also capable of forming wood.
This option is incorrect because, as mentioned above, both dicots and gymnosperms are capable of forming wood.

questions.explanation: Most seed plants increase their diameter through secondary growth, producing wood and bark. Monocots (and some dicots) have lost this ability, and so do not produce wood.

questions.text: The process of aging:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Can be stopped down by adequate sleep
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The process of aging cannot be completely stopped, but it can be slowed down by various factors including regular exercise, healthy diet, adequate sleep, stress management, and avoiding harmful habits such as smoking and excessive alcohol consumption.

options.text: Can be stopped down by regular meals
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The process of aging cannot be completely stopped, but it can be slowed down by various factors including regular exercise, healthy diet, adequate sleep, stress management, and avoiding harmful habits such as smoking and excessive alcohol consumption.

options.text: Cannot be slowed down in any way
options.explanation: It is factually incorrect. The process of aging cannot be completely stopped, but it can be slowed down by various factors including regular exercise, healthy diet, adequate sleep, stress management, and avoiding harmful habits such as smoking and excessive alcohol consumption.

options.text: Can be slowed by adequate sleep and regular meals
options.explanation: The process of aging cannot be completely stopped, but it can be slowed down by various factors including regular exercise, healthy diet, adequate sleep, stress management, and avoiding harmful habits such as smoking and excessive alcohol consumption. So, this is the correct option.

questions.explanation: Although exercise is the best way to slow down the process of aging, adopting a diet full of wholesome, nutritious, and fresh foods can keep your body functioning at optimal levels as well as can also slow down the process of aging.

questions.text: The reason plants are considered totipotent is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Each cell has full genetic potential of the organism
options.explanation: Each cell has the full genetic potential of the organism. This statement is true for totipotent cells in plants. Each cell contains the complete genetic information necessary to develop into any type of cell or tissue in the organism.

options.text: A single cell could become a complete plant
options.explanation: A single cell could become a complete plant. This statement is also true for totipotent cells. In plant tissue culture, a single plant cell can be isolated and grown in a nutrient-rich medium to regenerate into a whole plant. This ability of a single cell to give rise to an entire plant demonstrates the totipotency of plant cells.

options.text: Because they have lesser potential for tissue culturing
options.explanation: Because they have lesser potential for tissue culturing, this statement is incorrect. Plants are considered totipotent precisely because they have a high potential for tissue culturing. Totipotent cells can be used in tissue culture techniques to regenerate whole plants from small plant parts, such as explants or single cells.

Each cell has the full genetic potential of the organism, and a single cell could become a complete plant. The term "totipotent" means that a single cell has the ability to regenerate an entire plant. This is possible because each plant cell contains the full genetic potential of the organism. This means that under the right conditions, a single cell can be stimulated to divide and differentiate into all of the different cell types that make up the plant. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Each cell has the full genetic potential of the organism, and a single cell could become a complete plant. The term "totipotent" means that a single cell has the ability to regenerate an entire plant. This is possible because each plant cell contains the full genetic potential of the organism. This means that under the right conditions, a single cell can be stimulated to divide and differentiate into all of the different cell types that make up the plant.

questions.text: _ cells store surplus food and _ cells produce new cells for growth and development of the plant:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parenchyma cells are thin-walled, unspecialized cells that are responsible for storing and synthesizing food in plants. Meristematic cells are undifferentiated cells that are capable of dividing and producing new cells for growth and development. Hence, this option is correct.

Sclerenchymatous cells: Sclerenchymatous cells are a type of plant tissue that provides mechanical support and strength to the plant. They have thick cell walls due to the presence of lignin, which makes them rigid and non-flexible. Sclerenchyma cells are found in the stems, roots, and other parts of the plant where rigidity is required. Chlorenchymatous cells are specialized plant cells that contain chloroplasts. Chloroplasts are responsible for photosynthesis, the process by which plants convert sunlight into energy. Chlorenchyma cells are primarily found in the leaves and are involved in photosynthesis.

Phloem is a complex tissue in plants that is responsible for the transport of sugars, nutrients, and other organic compounds. It consists of different cell types, including sieve elements and companion cells. Sieve elements are responsible for conducting sugars, while companion cells provide support and metabolic functions. Meristematic cells are undifferentiated cells that give rise to new cells for plant growth and development.
Parenchymatous cells, as mentioned before, are involved in storing surplus food in plants. Chlorenchymatous cells contain chloroplasts and are responsible for photosynthesis in plants.

questions.explanation: Parenchyma cells are thin-walled, unspecialized cells that are responsible for storing and synthesizing food in plants. Meristematic cells are undifferentiated cells that are capable of dividing and producing new cells for growth and development.

questions.text: What is the phenomenon shown in the following picture?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: It's not correct since abscission is the natural process in which plants shed their leaves and animals shed their skin.
Is the word synynom for aging.
Is the phenomenon in which the apical buds keep on growing but the lateral buds stop their growt0h resulting in apical dominance.
Ripening is the complex process of a fruit developing from its mature, green stage to a fully edible state, characterized by changes in color, flavor, aroma, and texture.

questions.explanation: As it can be seen in the diagram above the apical end is active and the lateral end is dormant. The apical bud is active, growing, and dominant as it can be seen growing far more than lateral buds. Therefore it can be said there is apical dominance in this plant.Option A is wrong because abscission is the natural detachment of parts of a plant, typically dead leaves and ripe fruit. Option B is wrong because of senescence, the condition or process of deterioration with age. Option D is wrong because ripening refers to the maturation of fruits.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a function of Abscisic acid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Inhibits stem and root growth during drought
options.explanation: Abscisic acid inhibits stem and root growth notably during physiological stress, e.g. drought, and waterlogging.
Abscisic acid promotes closing of stomata under conditions of water stress (wilting).
Abscisic acid promotes flowering in short day plants, and inhibits in long day plants

options.text: Promotes bud initiation during growth season.
options.explanation: This option is correct because this is the function of cytokinis and gibberellin, not abscisic acid's. ABA inhibits plant growth

questions.explanation: Abscisic acid is the plant hormone, which is responsible for the dormancy of seeds and inhibition of seeds. It stimulates the closure of stomata and inhibits shoot growth and etc.

questions.text: Nutrition to egg in ovary is provided by which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Germ cells refer to the cells that give rise to gametes (sperm in males and eggs in females). While germ cells are involved in the process of forming eggs, they are not the cells directly providing nutrition to the egg.
There is no specific cell type known as "milk cells" in the context of egg development in the ovary. The term is not applicable to this biological process.

Nutrition for the egg in the ovary is primarily provided by the follicle cells. These cells surround and support the developing egg within the ovarian follicle. They supply nutrients to the growing oocyte and help facilitate its maturation.
This option is not correct. Nutrition to the egg in the ovary is primarily provided by the follicle cells, not germ cells or imaginary "milk cells."

questions.explanation: Graafian follicle is a fluid-filled sac that surrounds the developing egg. It is located in the ovary and it is responsible for providing the egg with nutrients and protection.

questions.text: Growth and development of plant cells is the role of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Parenchyma cells are a type of plant cell that are involved in various functions such as storage, photosynthesis, and secretion. They have thin cell walls, large central vacuoles, and flexible shapes.
Chlorenchyma cells are a type of parenchyma cell that contain chloroplasts. They are specialized for photosynthesis, converting light energy into chemical energy for the plant.

The growth and development of plant cells are primarily the role of meristematic cells. Meristematic cells are a group of undifferentiated, actively dividing cells found in specific regions of plant tissues called meristems.
Sclerenchyma cells are plant cells that provide support, strength, and rigidity to plant tissues. They have thick, lignified secondary cell walls that make them tough and durable.

questions.explanation: The growth and development of plant cells are primarily the role of meristematic cells. Meristematic cells are a group of undifferentiated, actively dividing cells found in specific regions of plant tissues called meristems.

questions.text: The living phloem, cork and cork cambium is collectively called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Periderm consists of cork, cork cambium, and phelloderm, which are parts of the protective outer layer but do not include phloem.
Protoderm is a type of primary meristem that differentiates into the epidermis, not related to phloem or cork.
Periblem is involved in forming the cortex in roots and stems, not the outer protective structures like bark.
Bark is the correct term for the collective structure that includes the living phloem, cork, and cork cambium. It serves as a protective layer for the tree.

questions.explanation: The living phloem, cork and cork cambium is collectively called Bark.

questions.text: To the end of the first trimesters, the embryo can now technically describe as a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A zygote is the initial cell formed when a sperm cell fertilizes an egg cell. It is the earliest developmental stage and not applicable by the end of the first trimester.
An infant refers to a child post-birth, generally from 0 to 12 months. At the end of the first trimester, the embryo is not yet born and thus cannot be described as an infant.
A toddler is a child between 12 and 36 months of age. This term describes a later developmental stage that occurs after infancy.
This option is correct because, by the end of the first trimester, the embryo has developed into a fetus, signifying the transition from the embryonic stage to the fetal stage of development.

questions.explanation: At the conclusion of the first trimester, the embryo undergoes significant development and is officially termed a fetus. This transition marks the end of the embryonic stage, as critical structures have begun forming, preparing for further growth and development. Other options such as zygote, infant, and toddler refer to different stages of development, either too early in pregnancy or post-birth, making them incorrect in this context.

questions.text: Acetabularia crenulata has a _ shaped cap:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acetabularia crenulata is known for its irregular-shaped cap, which distinguishes it from other species like Acetabularia mediterranea.
While Acetabularia mediterranea has an umbrella-shaped cap, Acetabularia crenulata does not.
The term 'regular' does not accurately describe the shape of Acetabularia crenulata's cap, which is irregular.
A disc-shaped cap would suggest a more uniform and rounded shape, which does not match the characteristics of Acetabularia crenulata.

questions.explanation: Acetabularia is a genus of unicellular green algae, notable for its large size and distinctive cap shapes. Specifically, Acetabularia crenulata features an irregular-shaped cap, which is a key characteristic for identifying this species. In contrast, Acetabularia mediterranea has an umbrella-shaped cap. Understanding these differences is crucial for distinguishing between species in the Acetabularia genus. The incorrect options represent shapes that do not correspond with the known morphology of Acetabularia crenulata.

questions.text: Implantation of the embryo takes place in which week of pregnancy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During the first week of pregnancy, fertilization occurs. After an egg is released from the ovary and is fertilized by sperm, it forms a single-cell structure called a zygote. The zygote begins to divide rapidly as it travels through the fallopian tube towards the uterus.

In the second week of pregnancy, the zygote continues to divide and forms a ball of cells called a blastocyst. The blastocyst further develops as it moves down the fallopian tube and towards the uterus. By the end of the second week implantation of the blastocyst in the endometrium begins.

Implantation begins during the 2nd week. In the third week, a two-layered embryonic disc transforms into a three-layered embryo in which primary germ layers ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm are present.
Implantation occurs during the 2nd week not the 4th.

questions.explanation: In the second week of pregnancy, the zygote continues to divide and forms a ball of cells called a blastocyst. The blastocyst further develops as it moves down the fallopian tube and towards the uterus. By the end of the second week implantation of the blastocyst in the endometrium begins.

questions.text: In chick, development gives rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In chick, development gives rise to ectoderm and endoderm. In chick embryos, the ectoderm and endoderm cells ultimately give rise to different tissues and organs. Ectoderm cells generate the skin and neural tissue. Endoderm cells become the lining of the gastrointestinal and the respiratory tracts.

In chick, development gives rise to ectoderm and endoderm. In chick embryos, the ectoderm and endoderm cells ultimately give rise to different tissues and organs. Ectoderm cells generate the skin and neural tissue. Endoderm cells become the lining of the gastrointestinal and the respiratory tracts.

This option is incorrect. In chick, development gives rise to ectoderm and endoderm. In chick embryos, the ectoderm and endoderm cells ultimately give rise to different tissues and organs. Ectoderm cells generate the skin and neural tissue. Endoderm cells become the lining of the gastrointestinal and the respiratory tracts.

This option is incorrect. In chick, development gives rise to ectoderm and endoderm. In chick embryos, the ectoderm and endoderm cells ultimately give rise to different tissues and organs. Ectoderm cells generate the skin and neural tissue. Endoderm cells become the lining of the gastrointestinal and the respiratory tracts.

questions.explanation: In chick, development gives rise to ectoderm and endoderm. In chick embryos, the ectoderm and endoderm cells ultimately give rise to different tissues and organs. Ectoderm cells generate the skin and neural tissue. Endoderm cells become the lining of the gastrointestinal and the respiratory tracts.

questions.text: Which one of the following generally acts as an antagonist to gibberellins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Zeatin is a natural plant hormone and a type of cytokinin. Cytokinins are a class of plant growth-regulating hormones that play essential roles in various aspects of plant growth and development.

Ethylene is a colourless, odourless gas that serves as a plant hormone and is involved in various physiological processes in plants. It plays a crucial role in regulating plant growth and development. Ethylene is often referred to as the "ripening hormone" because it accelerates the ripening of fruits. It triggers the conversion of starches into sugars, softening of the fruit, and the development of characteristic fruit aroma and flavour.

Abscisic Acid (ABA) is a plant hormone that plays a significant role in regulating various physiological processes in plants. ABA is involved in the plant's response to environmental stress, particularly water stress, and it helps plants adapt to adverse conditions.ABA triggers the closure of stomata, which are small openings on the surface of leaves and stems.

Indole-3-Acetic Acid (IAA) is a natural plant hormone and a type of auxin. Auxins are a class of plant growth regulators that play a key role in various aspects of plant growth and development. IAA promotes the elongation of plant cells, which is essential for the growth of stems and roots.

questions.explanation: The plant growth regulator that generally acts as an antagonist to gibberellins is abscisic acid (ABA). Gibberellins promote various growth processes in plants such as stem elongation, seed germination, and breaking of dormancy, while abscisic acid has the opposite effect. ABA induces dormancy in seeds and buds, inhibits germination, and helps plants withstand stress conditions like drought by closing stomata to reduce water loss. Therefore, while gibberellins stimulate active growth and development, abscisic acid suppresses these activities to conserve energy and protect the plant under unfavourable conditions. Hence, abscisic acid acts as a natural antagonist to gibberellins, maintaining a balance between growth and dormancy in plants.

questions.text: Concerning fertilization in hens, which statement is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. In hens, fertilization is internal. When the ovary releases an ovum (the yolk), it enters the infundibulum of the oviduct, where sperm from the male rooster may be present. The sperm fertilizes the ovum inside the female reproductive tract, long before the egg gets its albumen, membranes, and shell. Thus, fertilization never occurs outside the body.

options.text: Outside the albumen, there is only one shell membrane and no calcareous layer
options.explanation: This statement is also incorrect. A hen’s egg is a highly protected structure with several layers. Surrounding the yolk is the albumen (egg white), and outside the albumen are two shell membranes—an inner and an outer membrane. Beyond these membranes lies the hard calcareous shell, which is primarily composed of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) and gives the egg its rigidity. Therefore, the statement mentioning “one shell membrane and no calcareous layer” is false.

options.text: The egg of a hen is laid immediately after fertilization occurs
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect as well. After fertilization, the egg takes approximately 24 to 26 hours to travel through the oviduct. During this journey, layers of albumen, shell membranes, and the hard shell are secreted around the fertilized ovum. Only after the complete egg is formed in the uterus region does the hen lay it. Hence, the egg is not laid immediately after fertilization but nearly a day later.

options.text: Cleavage in hen is of discoidal type
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The hen’s egg contains a large amount of yolk (it is telolecithal), with the cytoplasm and nucleus confined to a small disc-like region on top called the germinal disc. Because of this large yolk, cleavage cannot cut through the entire egg; it occurs only in that disc-shaped cytoplasmic area. This restricted form of cleavage is known as discoidal meroblastic cleavage, typical of birds, reptiles, and fish.

questions.explanation: Cleavage in a hen is of the discoidal type because the egg contains a large amount of yolk, which prevents complete division of the egg. During early development, only a small portion of cytoplasm on top of the yolk, called the blastodisc, undergoes cell division. The rest of the yolk remains undivided. This type of cleavage is typical of birds, reptiles, and other animals with yolk-rich eggs.

questions.text: Muscles, the axial skeleton, and connective tissue arise from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mesomeres are a term used in embryology to describe a specific type of cell or cell group that forms during early embryonic development, particularly during cleavage.

Metameres, or somites, are segmental structures found in the embryonic development of many animals, especially chordates. They are formed during the segmentation process, where the embryo divides into repeated segments. In humans, mesodermal blocks called somitomeres develop into somites, which differentiate into essential tissues, including the axial skeleton, skeletal muscles, and dermis. Somites are regionally specialized; for instance, those in the head form facial muscles, while those in the trunk develop into vertebrae. This segmentation is crucial for creating a coordinated body plan and has been significant in evolution, allowing for diverse adaptations across ecological niches.

Hypomeres refer to specific regions of the embryonic mesoderm in certain animals, particularly arthropods. The mesoderm, one of the three primary germ layers, is divided into two main regions: the hypomere (ventral/lower portion) and the epimere (dorsal/upper portion). Each hypomere corresponds to a body segment, such as thoracic or abdominal segments in insects, playing a crucial role in the development of muscles, the circulatory system, and connective tissues. The organization and function of hypomeres can vary among different animal groups, primarily applying to arthropods and some invertebrates.

In vertebrate embryonic development, the muscles, axial skeleton, and connective tissues arise from the epimere, also known as the paraxial mesoderm. The mesoderm, which is the middle germ layer of the embryo, differentiates into three main regions: the epimere (paraxial mesoderm), mesomere (intermediate mesoderm), and hypomere (lateral plate mesoderm). The epimere lies on either side of the developing neural tube and segments into blocks of tissue called somites. Each somite further differentiates into three parts: the sclerotome, which forms the axial skeleton, such as the vertebrae and ribs; the myotome, which gives rise to skeletal muscles; and the dermatome, which develops into the connective tissue of the skin (dermis). Hence, the epimere plays a crucial role in forming the body’s musculoskeletal and connective structures.

questions.explanation: In vertebrate embryos, the mesoderm differentiates into three main regions: the epimere (paraxial mesoderm), mesomere (intermediate mesoderm), and hypomere (lateral plate mesoderm). Each region gives rise to specific tissues in the developing body. The epimere, located nearest to the neural tube, segments into somites that form the axial skeleton, skeletal muscles, and connective tissue of the skin. The mesomere, lying between the epimere and hypomere, develops into excretory and reproductive organs such as the kidneys and gonads. The hypomere, the outermost part of the mesoderm, splits into two layers that form the body cavity lining, circulatory structures, and walls of the gut. Thus, among these three regions, only the epimere gives rise to the muscles, axial skeleton, and connective tissue, making it the correct answer.

questions.text: The phenomenon in which one embryonic tissue influences the other is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Morulation is a crucial early stage in embryonic development that occurs after fertilization and before the blastocyst forms in mammals. Typically happening within the first few days post-fertilization, this process marks the transition from a single-celled zygote to a multicellular embryo.

Embryonic induction is a key concept in developmental biology, referring to how one group of cells influences the development of neighbouring cells. This process involves signalling molecules like growth factors and morphogens that instruct adjacent cells to differentiate into specific types or structures.

Neurulation is a critical early stage in the embryonic development of vertebrates, including humans, where the neural tube is formed. The neural tube is the precursor to the central nervous system, which includes the brain and spinal cord.

Cleavage, in the context of embryology and development, refers to a series of rapid and successive cell divisions that occur in the early stages of the development of a multicellular organism, typically following fertilization. These cell divisions are essential for the formation of a multicellular structure from a single-celled zygote and give rise to a cluster of smaller, undifferentiated cells called blastomeres.

questions.explanation: Embryonic induction is a key concept in developmental biology, referring to how one group of cells influences the development of neighbouring cells. This process involves signalling molecules like growth factors and morphogens that instruct adjacent cells to differentiate into specific types or structures.

questions.text: The apical meristem of the root is present:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Only in the region of maturation
options.explanation: The region of maturation is where root cells differentiate into specialized types, such as xylem, phloem, and root hairs. Since cell division does not occur here, this region does not contain the apical meristem.

options.text: Only in the region of elongation
options.explanation: The region of elongation lies just above the meristematic zone and is where cells increase in size but no longer divide actively. Therefore, this region is not where the apical meristem is located.

options.text: Between the root cap and the region of elongation
options.explanation: This is the correct answer. The root apical meristem is found between the root cap (which protects it) and the region of elongation. It consists of actively dividing cells responsible for the primary growth and elongation of the root.

options.text: At the tip of the root cap
options.explanation: The root cap covers and protects the apical meristem but does not contain meristematic cells itself. The tip of the root cap mainly consists of protective cells, not dividing ones. Therefore, the apical meristem is not present at the tip of the root cap.

questions.explanation: The apical meristem of the root is located between the root cap and the region of elongation. It is made up of actively dividing cells that help the root grow in length. The root cap protects the meristem as the root pushes through the soil. The region of elongation lies just above it, where cells enlarge but do not divide. Above that is the region of maturation, where cells differentiate into specialized cells. Only the zone between the root cap and the region of elongation contains the root apical meristem.

questions.text: Chlorenchyma is known to develop in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Xylem is a complex permanent tissue responsible for the transport of water and minerals from the roots to other parts of the plant. It consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem fibres, and xylem parenchyma. Since it is mainly concerned with conduction and support, it does not contain chloroplasts, so chlorenchyma does not develop here.

Phloem is another complex tissue that transports food and organic nutrients throughout the plant. It includes sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibers, and phloem parenchyma. These cells are not photosynthetic and therefore do not form chlorenchyma.

This is the correct answer. The mesophyll is the inner tissue of a leaf found between the upper and lower epidermis. It consists of palisade and spongy parenchyma, both of which contain chloroplasts. These chloroplast-rich parenchyma cells are known as chlorenchyma, and they carry out photosynthesis.

The pericycle is a layer of cells present just inside the endodermis in roots and sometimes in stems. It plays a role in lateral root formation and provides support but lacks chloroplasts. Therefore, chlorenchyma does not develop in the pericycle.

questions.explanation: Chlorenchyma is a type of parenchyma tissue containing chloroplasts that perform photosynthesis. It develops in the mesophyll region of leaves, which includes the palisade and spongy parenchyma cells. Other tissues, such as xylem, phloem, and pericycle, are involved in transport or support and do not contain chloroplasts. Therefore, chlorenchyma develops only in the mesophyll of the leaf.

questions.text: An organized and differentiated cellular structure having cytoplasm but no nucleus is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vessels are elongated, dead cells with open end walls that allow for the rapid transport of water and minerals throughout the plant. They have a nucleus when they are first formed, but they lose it as they mature.
Xylem parenchyma are living cells found in xylem, the water-transporting tissue of plants. They have a nucleus and other organelles, and they are involved in storage and support.

This option is correct because Sieve cells/sieve tube element are living and thin walled. Mature sieve tube elements are enucleated living cells. Central vacuole is present in each sieve cells/sieve tube element and around the central vacuole thin layer of cytoplasm is present.
Tracheids are elongated, dead cells with lignified cell walls that provide structural support to the plant. They have a nucleus when they are first formed, but they lose it as they mature.

questions.explanation: An organised and differentiated cellular structure having cytoplasm but no nucleus are sieve tubes. The sieve tubes are living cells. Their walls are thicker than surrounding parenchyma cells. Sometimes they havea special, shining nacreous thickening. Cytoplasm occurs in the form of thin lining enclosing a big central vacuole.

questions.text: What is formed when the blastula folds inwards?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As a consequence of cleavage, the embryo becomes a rounded mass of closely packed blastomeres, which resembles a mulberry and therefore is called morula.
Blastula contains a fluid filled cavity known as the segmentation cavity or blastocoel.

The blastopore is the opening in the developing embryo during gastrulation, which eventually forms either the mouth or anus in many animals, defining their body's basic orientation. Its fate varies among different species.

The next stage followed by blastula is called gastrula and process of its formation is called gastrulation. During this process, cells of blastula migrate inwards and their rearrangment at different locations in the embryo occurs. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: The next stage followed by blastula is called gastrula and process of its formation is called gastrulation. During this process, cells of blastula migrate inwards and their rearrangment at different locations in the embryo occurs. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.text: The eggs of a chick is laid at which of the following stages?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The blastula is an early stage in embryonic development, characterized by a hollow ball of cells. In birds like chickens, the embryo reaches the blastula stage before the egg is laid. This stage is crucial because it sets the foundation for further development once the egg is laid and incubation begins. The embryo at this stage is often referred to as a blastoderm in avian species

The morula stage occurs earlier than the blastula. It is a solid ball of cells formed after several rounds of cell division (cleavage) following fertilization. The morula transitions into the blastula stage as the cells continue to divide and a cavity forms within. The egg is not laid during the morula stage.

The gastrula stage follows the blastula and involves significant cell movement and differentiation, forming the three primary germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. The chick's egg is usually laid before reaching the gastrula stage, making this an incorrect option.

Neurulation is a later stage of development where the neural tube, which will eventually become the brain and spinal cord, begins to form. This stage occurs after gastrulation and well after the egg has been laid, so it is not the correct stage for when the egg is laid.

questions.explanation: The egg is laid when the embryo has reached the blastula stage, which contains a fluid-filled cavity and is the stage right before gastrulation begins after incubation starts.

questions.text: All animals begin life as a _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not accurate, as the fertilized egg is initially a single cell.

All animals, from simple sponges to complex humans, begin their lives as a single fertilized egg cell, also known as a zygote. This zygote is formed when a sperm fertilizes an egg cell, resulting in a single cell with a unique combination of genetic material from both parents.From this single cell, the embryo develops through various stages, including:1. Cleavage: The zygote divides into multiple cells.2. Blastula: A hollow ball of cells forms.3. Gastrula: The blastula undergoes invagination, forming a multilayered embryo.So, while all animals do pass through the blastula and gastrula stages, they all begin life as a single fertilized egg.
While the blastula is a stage in embryonic development, it is not the starting point for animal life.
Similarly, the gastrula is a stage in embryonic development, but not the beginning of animal life.

questions.explanation: All animals, from simple sponges to complex humans, begin their lives as a single fertilized egg cell, also known as a zygote. This zygote is formed when a sperm fertilizes an egg cell, resulting in a single cell with a unique combination of genetic material from both parents.From this single cell, the embryo develops through various stages, including:1. Cleavage: The zygote divides into multiple cells.2. Blastula: A hollow ball of cells forms.3. Gastrula: The blastula undergoes invagination, forming a multilayered embryo.So, while all animals do pass through the blastula and gastrula stages, they all begin life as a single fertilized egg.

questions.text: Fertilized ovum is implanted and undergoes further development in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ovaries are responsible for the production of eggs and the release of mature eggs during ovulation. Fertilization occurs in the oviduct, not the ovary.

After fertilization, the fertilized ovum, also known as the zygote, undergoes further development and implantation in the uterus. The uterus, or womb, is a female reproductive organ located in the pelvic region. It provides a suitable environment for the implantation of the fertilized ovum and supports the development of the embryo or foetus.
Fertilization occurs in the oviduct, specifically in the ampulla region, where the sperm and egg meet. However, further development of the fertilized ovum occurs after implantation in the uterus.

The cervix is the lower part of the uterus that connects it to the vagina. It plays a role in the passage of menstrual blood and the delivery of the baby during childbirth but is not the site of implantation or further development of the fertilized ovum.

questions.explanation: After fertilization, the fertilized ovum, also known as the zygote, undergoes further development and implantation in the uterus. The uterus, or womb, is a female reproductive organ located in the pelvic region. It provides a suitable environment for the implantation of the fertilized ovum and supports the development of the embryo or foetus.

questions.text: Which of the following is not true regarding the phenomenon of aging?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Cells undergo mitosis as a part of growth of the body
options.explanation: Correct. This is true because mitosis is essential for the growth and repair of tissues throughout an organism's life.

options.text: After a number of cell divisions, cells become differentiated
options.explanation: Correct. Differentiation leads to specialized cell functions, a normal part of development.

options.text: After maximum metabolic activity, cells in the body start deteriorating
options.explanation: Correct. This is a natural part of aging, as cells lose efficiency and regenerative abilities over time.

options.text: The resistance to negative environmental factors remains the same throughout life
options.explanation: Incorrect. Resistance to infections, oxidative stress, and environmental toxins declines with age due to weakened immune and cellular repair mechanisms.

questions.explanation: Resistance to infections, oxidative stress, and environmental toxins declines with age due to weakened immune and cellular repair mechanisms.

questions.text: In humans, the ability of regeneration is restricted to the following organ
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Bones can heal but do not have full regenerative ability.
The skin has a high regenerative capacity, constantly replacing old cells and healing wounds efficiently.
Nerve regeneration is extremely limited, especially in the central nervous system.
Muscles have some regenerative potential but not as much as skin.

questions.explanation: The skin has a high regenerative capacity, constantly replacing old cells and healing wounds efficiently.

questions.text: Developing seeds are a rich source of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Estrogen is a hormone primarily found in animals, not in seeds. It is produced by ovaries in females.
Cytokinins promote cell division and growth in developing seeds.
Auxins help regulate plant growth and are found in developing seeds.
Developing seeds contain both cytokinins and auxins, playing vital roles in plant growth.

questions.explanation: Developing seeds contain both cytokinins and auxins, playing vital roles in plant growth. Cytokinins are a class of plant hormones that promote cell division and growth, particularly in the root and shoot meristems while Auxin is a group of plant hormones that regulate cell growth and development, including cell elongation

questions.text: Which one of the following is a derivative of indole acetic acid or its variant?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Auxins, like indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), promote plant growth. Auxin is a group of plant hormones that regulate cell growth and development, including cell elongation. It is produced in the tips of shoots and roots and plays a crucial role in plant responses to environmental stimuli like light (phototropism) and gravity (gravitropism)
Cytokinins promote cell division particularly in the root and shoot meristems but are not derived from indole acetic acid.
Cytochromes are involved in electron transport, not plant hormones.

Abscisic acid (ABA) is a plant hormone that regulates many aspects of plant growth and development, including seed and bud dormancy, stress responses like drought, and stomatal closure. It is not derived from indole acetic acid.

questions.explanation: Auxins, like indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), promote plant growth. Auxin is a group of plant hormones that regulate cell growth and development, including cell elongation. It is produced in the tips of shoots and roots and plays a crucial role in plant responses to environmental stimuli like light (phototropism) and gravity (gravitropism)
